_comment: Complete example output from /forkfeed on kondulak10/forkfeed.app commit d556ed5. This file covers all 8 card types with annotations. Study the patterns here before generating new content.

forks._comment_fork: One fork per repo. Title is the PROJECT name (not owner/repo). Description is what the project IS (not what the skill does). imageSrc reuses Card 0's FULL_IMAGE imageSrc. feedIds are newest-first.
forks.title: Forkfeed
forks.description: A TikTok-style content app replacing doom scrolling with feeds you actually control.

feeds._comment_feed: One feed per commit. Title is a human-readable headline (max 60 chars), NOT the raw commit message. Description starts with 'Mon DD:' then impact. imageSrc matches Card 0's cover. mode/scrollDirection/engagement are always these values.
feeds.title: Spring cleaning and a health boost
feeds.description: Mar 27: Deleted three outdated files, fixed doc paths, and watched the project health score climb from 73% to 78%.

cards._comment_card0: CARD 0: Explain like I'm 5 (order 0). Standard block pattern: CONTENT_IMAGE (wide) -> CONTENT_TITLE -> CONTENT_TEXT. 3-6 detail variants. Tone: friendly, absurd analogies, zero jargon. Each detail variant explains a different aspect of the commit.

variants._comment: variant[0] is ALWAYS FULL_IMAGE. Title is section name in sentence case. Subtitle is a hook (max 200 chars) that makes you want to swipe right. This card has 5 variants total (1 cover + 4 detail).
variants.title: Explain like I'm 5
variants.subtitle: You threw out old books, art instructions, and an empty folder. The house feels lighter already.

variants._comment: variant[1+] are CONTENT. All detail variants in this card share the SAME backgroundSrc. CONTENT_IMAGE imageSrc MUST differ from the cover imageSrc (never reuse cover image in details).

blocks.text:
Imagine your bedroom has a bookshelf full of old books you don't read, a drawer stuffed with last year's art project instructions, and an empty folder just sitting there collecting dust.

Today, you cleaned all of that out. Three files got removed: a reading list of 20 books (books.md), a massive set of AI art prompts for onboarding images (onboarding.md), and an empty tasks file that was literally zero bytes. Just vibes, no content.

You know when your family moves the snacks from the kitchen cupboard to the pantry, but the note on the fridge still says "snacks are in the cupboard"?

That's what happened with the force-push skill docs. Content files moved from the `content/` folder to `manifests/` a while back, but the instructions still pointed to the old spot. Two lines changed, both paths now correct. Snacks located.

cards._comment_card1: CARD 1: The roast (order 1). Same standard block pattern. 3-6 detail variants. Tone: maximum cheekiness, standup comedy, swearing welcome. Mix sarcastic observations + fake ratings + bug radar. CONTENT_SUBTEXT for protips.

variants.title: The roast
variants.subtitle: 98 lines deleted, 19 added. Congratulations on achieving negative productivity.

variants._comment: Sarcastic observation variant. References actual code/files from the diff for maximum impact.

blocks.text:
So you decided to Marie Kondo your codebase. "Does this spark joy?" you whispered to books.md. It did not spark joy. Neither did 49 lines of ghost art prompts. Or an empty file called tasks.md that was literally zero bytes.

Bold of it to even exist. An empty markdown file in a git repo is like bringing an empty lunchbox to school and calling it meal prep.

variants._comment: Bug radar variant. Uses CONTENT_SUBTEXT for constructive protips wrapped in humor.

blocks.text:
No tests were harmed (or written) in the making of this commit. You deleted three files and changed two. The health report improved from 73% to 78% but that improvement happened independently, you just updated the HTML to reflect it.

So really, this commit is the messenger taking credit for someone else's work. And you still have 13 lint issues. But sure, celebrate the 78%.
Protip: the health report update is accurate, but automating it would prevent the manual copy-paste drift that got those paths wrong in the first place.

cards._comment_card2: CARD 2: Commit message, decoded (order 2). DIFFERENT block pattern: uses CONTENT_SOCIAL instead of CONTENT_IMAGE. Each variant is a different 'translator' persona with escalating absurdity. 2-4 detail variants.

variants.title: Commit message, decoded
variants.subtitle: 33 words to say 'I deleted stuff.' Let's see what you really meant.

variants._comment: CONTENT_SOCIAL replaces CONTENT_IMAGE for cards 2-3. avatarSrc uses a scene image. Source varies by persona (x, linkedin, threads).

blocks.text:
"I noticed we had a bunch of leftover files from when we were planning content and generating onboarding art. None of it's needed anymore. Also the force-push docs still said content/ instead of manifests/ which has been wrong for a while. Fixed that too while I was in here."

Honest, direct, and roughly 80% shorter than the actual commit message.

blocks.code:
Remove outdated content files including books,
onboarding prompts, and tasks; update force-push
skill documentation to reflect changes in file
paths for content uploads, enhancing clarity and
accuracy in the upload process.

variants._comment: Corporate speak persona. Source is always 'linkedin' for this style.

blocks.name: Director of Strategic Content Deprecation
blocks.subtitle: VP of File Lifecycle Management at ForkCorp

blocks.text:
Per our Q1 content lifecycle optimization initiative, legacy knowledge assets have been sunset to reduce cognitive overhead across the developer experience surface area.

Additionally, pathway references have been realigned to reflect our strategic migration from content-centric to manifest-oriented architecture, enhancing the discoverability and accuracy of our internal tooling documentation.

A cross-functional health assessment has been updated to reflect improved compliance metrics, demonstrating a 5-point uplift in our engineering quality index.

variants._comment: Military briefing persona. Source is 'x'. Escalating absurdity from the honest version above.

blocks.text:
SITREP 2033 HOURS. Three hostiles neutralized: BOOKS (reading list, 29 lines), ONBOARDING (art intel, 49 lines), TASKS (empty shell, 0 lines). Two friendly paths corrected in force-push documentation. Sector health assessment upgraded from 73 to 78 percent. Admin TypeScript errors reduced to zero.

Test execution time reduced by 82% in Expo sector. Zero collateral damage to operational systems. Requesting permission to push to main. Over.

cards._comment_card3: CARD 3: The LinkedIn post (order 3). Also uses CONTENT_SOCIAL (same as card 2). Source is ALWAYS 'linkedin'. Each variant is a different persona. CONTENT_SUBTEXT for fake engagement metrics. 2-4 detail variants.

variants.title: The LinkedIn post
variants.subtitle: You deleted three files. Time to update your personal brand.

variants._comment: Hustle bro persona. Name/subtitle are consistent across all generates. CONTENT_SUBTEXT shows fake reactions/comments.

blocks.name: Chad Gitpush
blocks.subtitle: 10x Engineer | Building in Public | DMs Open

blocks.text:
Day 1,247 of my coding journey.

Today I deleted 98 lines of code.

Most developers are ADDING lines. They're building. They're shipping. But I had the COURAGE to subtract.

Because real growth isn't about what you add. It's about what you have the DISCIPLINE to remove.

books.md? Gone. onboarding.md? Eliminated. An empty tasks file? Obliterated.

98 lines lighter. 100% more focused.

Like if you've ever deleted a file.
Comment 'PURGE' if you're ready to simplify.
47 reactions, 12 comments: "This. So much this." "Needed to hear this today." "The courage to delete > the courage to ship."

blocks.name: Alexandra Middleware
blocks.subtitle: VP of Forward Thinking at TechCorp

blocks.text:
Hot take: we don't have a technical debt problem. We have a HOARDING problem.

Today I removed three files from our repository. One was a reading list. One was AI art prompts. One was literally empty.

And you know what happened? The health score went up 5%.

Sometimes the bravest engineering decision is knowing when to let go. I call this the "Subtraction Principle" and I'm writing a 47-part thread about it.

Follow for more thoughts on digital minimalism in enterprise codebases.
89 reactions, 23 comments: "Subscribing for part 2." "Finally someone said it." "Can you do a webinar on this?"

cards._comment_card4: CARD 4: Statistics (order 4). Standard block pattern but primarily uses CONTENT_CODE for structured data instead of CONTENT_TEXT. Tone: deadpan humor meets data. 3-5 detail variants.

variants.title: Statistics
variants.subtitle: 5 files touched, 98 lines removed, and the health report is feeling optimistic.

variants._comment: Stats as CONTENT_CODE with language 'bash'. Structured data always goes in code blocks, not text blocks.

blocks.code:
Files changed:     5
Additions:        +19
Deletions:        -98
Net change:       -79 lines
Languages:         Markdown, HTML
Branch:            main
Deletion ratio:    84% of this commit is removing things

  books.md              -29 lines
  onboarding.md         -49 lines
  tasks.md                0 lines (empty)

MODIFIED:
  .claude/skills/
    force-push/SKILL.md  +2  -2
  report.html            +17 -18

cards._comment_card5: CARD 5: Learning moment (order 5). Standard block pattern PLUS CONTENT_BUTTON as the LAST block in every detail variant. The button always says 'Google it' and links to a Google search. 3-8 detail variants. Tone: teach with personality.

variants.title: Learning moment
variants.subtitle: File lifecycle management, health dashboards, and the art of AI prompt engineering.

variants._comment: Every Learning moment detail variant MUST end with CONTENT_BUTTON. Label is always 'Google it'. Target URL-encodes the topic (spaces become +).

blocks.text:
When you start a project, you create files that serve a purpose at that moment. Reading lists, image prompts, task trackers. But these files have a lifecycle: they're created, they serve their purpose, and then they linger. Forever.

Until someone cleans them up. This commit demonstrates healthy file lifecycle management: recognizing when planning artifacts have outlived their usefulness. Think of it as pruning dead branches so the tree can grow. The best codebases aren't the ones with the most files, they're the ones where every file earns its place.

variants._comment: Can include CONTENT_CODE when it helps explain the pattern. Button is still the LAST block.

blocks.text:
This commit fixes paths from `content/*.json` to `manifests/*.json`. This reflects a common pattern: as projects mature, they reorganize.

Content files started in a generic `content/` directory but were moved to `manifests/` to better describe their purpose. A manifest is a declarative description of what should exist, like a shipping manifest lists what's in a container. The `content/` folder was too vague. `manifests/` tells you immediately: this file declares the shape of feeds, forks, and cards.

blocks.code:
# Before (generic):
content/atomic-habits.json
content/psychology-of-money.json

# After (intent-revealing):
manifests/atomic-habits.json
manifests/psychology-of-money.json

cards._comment_card6: CARD 6: Alternatives (order 6). Standard block pattern. ONLY section where synthesized/hypothetical code is allowed. Frame constructively with a wink. 3-6 detail variants.

variants.title: Alternatives
variants.subtitle: 4 different approaches to cleaning house, from archiving to automation.

variants._comment: Synthesized code IS allowed here (hypothetical alternatives). CONTENT_CODE shows what COULD have been done, not what was done.

blocks.text:
Instead of deleting books.md and onboarding.md outright, you could move them to a `docs/archive/` directory. This preserves institutional knowledge while keeping the root clean.

Some teams use a `_deprecated/` folder with date suffixes. Future contributors might want to reference those AI prompts as templates or rediscover that reading list. Git history preserves everything, sure, but who actually digs through git log looking for deleted files?

blocks.code:
# Archive structure
docs/
  archive/
    2026-03/
      books.md
      onboarding.md

blocks.text:
The report.html is manually updated with hardcoded values. Every time someone fixes a type error or a test gets faster, a human has to open the HTML and edit the numbers.

You could automate this with a CI step or a script that runs all checks, captures exit codes and error counts, then renders an HTML template. The report would always reflect reality instead of whatever someone remembered to update last.

blocks.code:
#!/bin/bash
# generate-report.sh
EXPO_ERRORS=$(cd expo && npx tsc --noEmit 2>&1 | grep -c 'error TS')
SERVER_ERRORS=$(cd app-server && npx tsc --noEmit 2>&1 | grep -c 'error TS')
TEST_RESULT=$(cd expo && npx jest --json 2>/dev/null)

# Render template with real values
envsubst < report.template.html > report.html

cards._comment_card7: CARD 7: Quiz (order 7). UNIQUE block pattern: NO CONTENT_IMAGE, starts with CONTENT_TITLE then CONTENT_QUIZ. 10-15 detail variants (one question each). Mix easy/medium/hard. Cover every significant change.

variants.title: Quiz
variants.subtitle: 12 questions about a commit that's mostly about deleting things. Good luck.

variants._comment: Quiz variants: NO CONTENT_IMAGE. Blocks are CONTENT_TITLE ('Question N') then CONTENT_QUIZ. Options array has exactly 4 items, exactly one correct:true. Explanation is required and should teach.

blocks.question: Which files were deleted in this commit?
blocks.explanation: Three files were deleted: books.md (a reading list), onboarding.md (AI art prompts), and tasks.md (an empty file). report.html and SKILL.md were modified, not deleted.

variants._comment: Mix difficulty levels. This is a medium question requiring understanding of the numbers.

blocks.question: How many lines of code were deleted in total?
blocks.explanation: 98 lines were deleted across the three removed files and the modifications. The net change was -79 (98 deleted minus 19 added), but total deletions were 98.

variants._comment: Hard question requiring deep technical knowledge.

blocks.question: What caused the health score to improve from 7/10 to 8/10?

options.label: Admin TypeScript errors were fixed (2 to 0)
A CI/CD pipeline was set up

blocks.explanation: The admin panel had 2 TypeScript TS2307 errors referencing a missing content-backends register page module. These were fixed (likely in a prior change), bringing the check from FAIL to PASS.
